title: Towards a nuclear isomer quantum battery
abstract: Quantum batteries (QBs) -- quantum devices governed by the principles of quantum mechanics -- hold great promise for next-generation energy storage. However, most existing research efforts focus on atomic or molecular systems featuring low-energy and short-lived energy levels, leaving the exploitation of high-energy, ultra-stable nuclear energy levels for energy storage as a critical unaddressed challenge. Here, we propose an innovative design of referred to as nuclear isomer quantum batteries (NIQBs), whose energy storage unit is typically composed of two-level and three-level nuclei incorporating nuclear isomers. The charging dynamics is driven by the interaction between the nuclear system and x-ray free electron laser (XFEL). Compared to QBs based on atomic systems, NIQBs deliver substantial performance enhancements, with stored energy and average charging power enhanced by factors of $10^{1}$--$10^{6}$ and $10^{6}$--$10^{11}$, respectively, and a markedly extended lifetime range spanning from microseconds to $10^5$ years. Notably, the vast majority of NIQBs enable complete energy extraction as their excited-state lifetimes exceed the laser-nuclear interaction time, rendering spontaneous emission negligible. The proposed NIQBs framework is compatible with diverse nuclear systems, enabling tailored nucleus selection for varied operating conditions. Our results provide a feasible pathway toward realizing high-performance QBs with superior energy-storage efficiency.
summary: The paper proposes a new class of quantum batteries using nuclear isomers to achieve unprecedented energy density and power. By utilizing X-ray Free Electron Lasers to drive transitions in nuclear systems, these batteries can store much higher energy levels than atomic versions and maintain that energy for much longer periods.
main_problem: Existing quantum batteries based on atomic or molecular systems suffer from low energy density and rapid self-discharging due to short excited-state lifetimes.
main_result: Nuclear Isomer Quantum Batteries (NIQBs) can achieve energy storage and charging power enhancements of up to 10^6 and 10^11, respectively, compared to atomic systems, with lifetimes extending up to 10^5 years.
method: The study uses a density matrix approach with a master equation to model coherent population transfer (NCPT) and STIRAP driven by X-ray Free Electron Laser (XFEL) pulses.
model_or_system: The system consists of nuclear ensembles in two-level, Lambda-type, and ladder-type configurations using various isotopes (e.g., 193Ir, 117Sn, 129Xe). The Hamiltonian includes nuclear energy levels and interaction with an external XFEL field.
key_observables: Stored energy (E), average charging power (P), ergotropy (W), energy extraction ratio (R), and system purity (P).
important_parameters: Energy scales of 10^5 eV to hundreds of keV; charging power in the Watt scale; interaction times on the femtosecond scale; lifetimes from microseconds to 10^5 years.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes the use of XFEL as a charger and focuses on regimes where spontaneous emission is negligible for most isotopes due to long isomeric lifetimes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows schematic setups for two-level, Lambda, and ladder-type NIQBs. Supplemental figures include contour plots of energy and extraction ratio versus laser intensity and energy extraction plots.
paper_structure: Introduction of the NIQB concept, description of the nuclear energy level configurations, mathematical framework for charging dynamics and energy metrics, comparative analysis between nuclear and atomic systems, and discussion of optimization and limitations.
why_interesting: It presents a novel application of nuclear physics to quantum thermodynamics, demonstrating how extremely long-lived metastable states can overcome the decoherence and energy-density limits typical of atomic-scale quantum batteries.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the NIQBs setup. (a) Schematic of a two level nuclear system, composed of the ground state |1⟩and an excited state |2⟩(isomeric state) of the nucleus. All popula- tion in |1⟩denotes an empty battery, while complete popula- tion in |2⟩corresponds to a fully charged state. (b) Schematic of nuclear three-level Λ and (c) ladder-type schemes, consist- ing of the nuclear ground state |1⟩and two different energy levels of excited states |2⟩and |3⟩(isomeric state). All pop- ulation in |1⟩means an empty battery, and a fully charged state is achieved with complete population in |3⟩.

FIG. 2. (a) The pulse shape controlled by π-pulse, (b) the time evolution of the stored energy E(t) (in units of eV ), (c) the average charging power P(t) (in units of W), and (d) the population of the target state for three selected two-level nuclear systems: 193Ir,117Sn, and 113Cd.

FIG. 3. (a), (e) The pulse shape controlled by STIRAP, (b), (f) time evolution of the stored energy E(t) (in units of eV ), (c), (g) the average charging power P(t) (in units of W) and (d), (h) the population P3 of the target state |3⟩for selected three-level nuclear systems: Λ-type of 154Gd, 107Ag and 103Rh (a–d) and ladder-type of 129Xe, 108Ag and 186Re (e–h), respectively.

FIG. 4. The stable stored energy (in units of eV ) and the maximum average charging power (in units of W) of three- level NIQBs and QBs for typical nuclei and the corresponding atom, respectively. Z denotes the neutron number of a nu- cleus. Solid squares and solid circles correspond to the stored energy and average charging power of NIQBs, respectively; hollow squares and hollow circles denote the same parameters for QBs. Colors indicate the lifetimes of the nuclear isomeric states.
